summary: J. Edgar Hoover was a man who enjoyed tremendous respect and was one of the most powerful Americans in his lifetime. I believe he may have had too much power, as power can corrupt. Hoover and the FBI kept files on many political and civil rights figures, including me, because of articles I wrote that they didn't like. I also knew that Hoover supplied presidents from Franklin D. Roosevelt onward with political information on various people.

The FBI was an agency I and many others respected greatly, especially when I was young. I even applied for a clerical job with them after high school but chose newspapering instead. The FBI kept files on many people, including myself, due to articles I wrote that Hoover didn't like. They also investigated my father during his political career, including searching his farms and bank accounts in Cuba, Mexico, and Canada. However, they never found anything wrong with him.

Earlier this year, I filed a request under the Freedom of Information Act to obtain my own FBI file. It revealed that the FBI had kept a file on me because of articles I wrote that Hoover and the Bureau didn't like. The Act allowed me to see how political and civil rights figures were monitored. This experience gave me insight into the extent of government surveillance during those times.

I visited Japan in 1939 as a guest of the Japanese government. During the 1942 gubernatorial race when Ellis Arnall ran against my father, Arnall portrayed me as somewhat of a traitor for having gone to Japan, despite my combat service in the South Pacific. I suspect this visit may have triggered an FBI investigation on me, though I never cared much about it. This episode was a significant part of my early political challenges.

Ellis Arnall ran against my father in the 1942 Georgia gubernatorial race and portrayed me as somewhat of a traitor because I had visited Japan in 1939 as a guest of their government. This was despite my active combat service in the South Pacific during World War II. Arnall's portrayal was part of the political attacks against my family during that period.

I served in combat in the South Pacific during World War II. This service was significant, especially when political opponents like Ellis Arnall accused me of disloyalty for my earlier visit to Japan. My time in the South Pacific demonstrated my commitment to the country despite political attacks. It was a defining period in my life during the early 1940s.

The CIA, like the FBI, has had its morale adversely affected by disclosures about plots to assassinate foreign leaders and other covert activities. Senators returning from overseas have told me that the CIA's sources of information are drying up because of these revelations. Since the CIA relies heavily on foreign sources, these disclosures have hurt its efficiency. Still, I believe that in the long run, this cleansing will prevent such circumstances from recurring in our lifetime.

Franklin D. Roosevelt was a hard-boiled politician, probably one of the hardest in our history. During my father's lifetime, Roosevelt and my father were at odds, and Roosevelt's administration set the tax people on my father, investigating him for ten years. They searched his farms and bank accounts in Cuba, Mexico, and Canada but never found anything wrong. I also assumed that Hoover and the FBI supplied Roosevelt and subsequent presidents with political information on various people.

During the tax investigations of my father initiated under Franklin D. Roosevelt, the authorities examined his bank accounts in Cuba among other places. This was part of a thorough ten-year investigation into his finances, including farms and livestock. Despite this scrutiny, they never found anything wrong with him. Cuba was one of several international locations involved in this political probe.

Mexico was one of the countries where my father's bank accounts were investigated during the tax probe initiated by Roosevelt's administration. This was part of a broader investigation that lasted about ten years, including inspections of farms and livestock. Despite the extensive search, no wrongdoing was found. This scrutiny was a reflection of the hard-nosed political environment of the time.

Canada was also included in the investigation of my father's bank accounts during the tax probe that lasted about ten years. The government looked into all aspects of his finances, including farms and livestock. Despite the thoroughness of the investigation, no evidence of wrongdoing was found. This was part of the political pressures my family faced during that era.

The FBI moved in on the Long dynasty in Louisiana and sent several members to the penitentiary. This was part of their efforts to investigate political corruption. However, despite their actions against the Long family, they never found anything wrong with my father. The Long dynasty's troubles were well known during that period and contrasted with my father's experience.

Richard Nixon learned that politicians can't easily get into litigation over income taxes, as my father experienced. The political climate around Nixon was intense, especially leading up to his resignation. I also have thoughts on the Ford pardon of Nixon, which was controversial. While many Americans were indignant about the timing, I believe Gerald Ford acted honorably and that the pardon helped the country move on.

Gerald Ford made a grave political error in pardoning Richard Nixon before an indictment or trial, but from the country's standpoint, it was probably a good thing to get that issue behind us. I believe Ford is a completely honorable man and did not make any deal regarding the pardon. The pardon was controversial and left many Americans with ambivalent feelings, but it helped the nation move forward.

From the very outset, I thought the Vietnam War was a mistake. My patriotism is like Stephen Decatur's — my loyalty follows the flag wherever the troops are. When we committed troops, we should have gone all out to win; had we used all resources, we might have won in ninety days to six months. The war was a no-win proposition because the country and the President were not fully behind it, and our troops fought with one or both hands tied behind their backs.

I often think of Stephen Decatur's famous saying about patriotism: 'My country, in her intercourse with a foreign country, may she always be in the right, but my country right or wrong.' This reflects my own loyalty to the flag and the troops wherever they may be, even during difficult times like the Vietnam War. His words have guided my sense of duty and patriotism throughout my life.

There is currently a discussion about U.S. aid to Angola, with concerns it could become another Vietnam. I don't think the U.S. will commit troops there because we've learned from Vietnam. I believe it's a mistake to get involved in tribal wars in Africa, where nationalistic movements dominate and countries may turn Communist or dictatorships. We have nothing to gain by intervening in Angola or similar conflicts.

In Africa, when Russia moves in, we tend to move in as well, and vice versa, but I think this is a mistake. Under current conditions, no white power can gain any advantage there. The countries are intensely nationalistic and likely to become Communist or dictatorships. I believe the U.S. should avoid involvement unless our national security is directly threatened.

Race is not a major political issue in Georgia today except regarding forced busing, which most people, black and white, oppose as a denial of basic freedoms. When I considered running for governor in 1966, the reaction from Georgians was overwhelming, with thousands of communications supporting my potential candidacy. Georgia politics have always been complex, but issues like forced busing remain very volatile here.

Ernest Vandiver was a former governor of Georgia expected to run again in 1966 but was advised by his doctor not to because of serious heart trouble. He called me early one morning at Dulles Airport to urge me to come home and run for governor instead. Vandiver's health concerns and family responsibilities led to his decision, and his encouragement sparked a huge public response to my potential candidacy.

I met Ernest Vandiver at Dulles Airport early one morning in 1966 when he arrived to discuss his health and political future. Vandiver told me his doctor advised against running for governor due to serious heart trouble and asked me to consider running instead. This meeting was pivotal in my contemplation of returning to Georgia politics and led to a significant public reaction.
